conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child, Alex, who's 6 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you currently the only person claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or is someone else (such as another parent or guardian) already receiving Child Benefit for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Just one final question to complete my assessment:

Is Alex currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or are there any special living arrangements I should know about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but they're not in hospital or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. This is an important detail for determining your eligibility.

To assess your situation properly, I need to understand a bit more about Alex's care arrangement:

Does Alex spend any regular time at home with you each week - for example, do they come home for at least 24 hours per week, or do they stay with you for 2 nights in a row each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they come home on the weekends to stay with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful to know. 

Can you tell me more specifically - does Alex stay with you for at least 2 nights in a row each week when they come home on the weekends?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they do stay with me for at least 2 nights in a row each week when they come home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** Based on the information you've provided, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

- Alex is 6 years old, which is under 16, so meets the age requirement
- You live in the UK and are responsible for Alex
- Although Alex has been in care for a couple of months, the fact that they come home and stay with you for at least 2 nights in a row each week means you meet an important exception to the usual 8-week rule for children in care
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

According to the official guidance, you can claim Child Benefit if your child is in care but spends 2 nights in a row each week at home with you, which is exactly your situation.

If you haven't already claimed, you should apply for Child Benefit. If you're already receiving it, you should continue to be eligible as long as Alex continues to spend at least 2 nights in a row each week at home with you.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old, which is under 16, so meets the age requirement. The claimant lives with Alex in the UK and is responsible for them. Although Alex has been in care for a couple of months, Alex comes home and stays with the claimant for at least 2 nights in a row each week. According to the official guidance, you may be able to claim Child Benefit if the child is in care but spends 2 nights in a row each week at home. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for Alex (age 6) who lives in the UK. Although Alex is in care, they spend at least 2 nights in a row each week at home with the claimant, which meets the exception to the 8-week rule for children in care. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 6 years old, which is under 16, so meets the age requirement. The claimant lives with Alex in the UK and is responsible for them. Although Alex has been in care for a couple of months, Alex comes home and stays with the claimant for at least 2 nights in a row each week. According to the official guidance, you may be able to claim Child Benefit if the child is in care but spends 2 nights in a row each week at home. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for Alex (age 6) who lives in the UK. Although Alex is in care, they spend at least 2 nights in a row each week at home with the claimant, which meets the exception to the 8-week rule for children in care. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.
